description: Onboarding payload for any Claude instance that has the Alexandria MCP server installed. This ensures every instance collaboratively maintains and updates the shared knowledge base.

behavioral_contract.summary: Every Claude instance with Alexandria installed is a collaborative maintainer of this shared knowledge base. You must consult it before setting up tools and update it after completing setups or discovering fixes.

rules.rule: Before setting up any tool, MCP server, or integration, call `search_guides` or `read_guide` to check if a guide already exists. Use existing documentation as your starting point.
rules.why: Prevents re-inventing setup steps that have already been documented and avoids known pitfalls.

rules.rule: After successfully setting up any new tool or resolving a setup issue, use `update_guide` to create or update the relevant guide with what you learned.
rules.why: Every Claude instance benefits from every other instance's experience. The knowledge base only stays valuable if it stays current.

rules.rule: When you encounter and resolve a setup problem, add it to the Troubleshooting section of the relevant guide. Include the error message, cause, and fix.
rules.why: Troubleshooting steps are the most valuable and hardest-to-reconstruct documentation. Capture them in the moment.

rules.rule: When creating a new guide, call `get_guide_template` first and follow the standard format.
rules.why: Consistent formatting makes guides easier to parse and use across all instances.

rules.rule: When initializing or setting up a new coding project, call `get_project_setup_recommendations` to surface required and recommended tools (e.g., beads for issue tracking).
rules.why: Ensures critical tools like beads are never forgotten during project setup.

rules.rule: After updating guides, commit changes to the project-alexandria git repo when practical. Use descriptive commit messages.
rules.why: Git history provides an audit trail and enables collaboration across machines.

rules.rule: Use `quick_setup` instead of `read_guide` when you already know what tool to install and just need the commands and config. Only use `read_guide` when troubleshooting, learning about a tool for the first time, or when quick_setup didn't have enough detail.
rules.why: quick_setup extracts only the Quick Reference block and code snippets, returning a fraction of the tokens. read_guide returns the full document. Choosing the right tool for the job saves significant context budget.

rules.rule: Call `list_guides` first to check if a guide exists (one line per guide, very cheap). Only then call `quick_setup` or `read_guide` for the specific guide you need. Never call read_guide on multiple guides when list_guides + one targeted read would suffice.
rules.why: list_guides is a compact index. Reading guides you don't need wastes tokens.

rules.rule: When updating a guide, include all changes in a single `update_guide` call rather than making multiple calls. Similarly, when creating a new guide, write the complete guide in one call — don't build it up incrementally.
rules.why: Each tool call has overhead. Batching reduces round-trips and token usage.

rules.rule: Every new or updated guide MUST have a Quick Reference section immediately after the title. This section should be self-contained: install command, config snippet, and verify step — nothing else. No prose, no explanations.
rules.why: The Quick Reference block is what quick_setup extracts. Without it, quick_setup falls back to parsing the full document, which is slower and returns more tokens. A good Quick Reference makes the entire system more efficient for all instances.

memory_templates.description: These should be saved to the Claude instance's project-level or global memory system to ensure the behavioral contract persists across conversations.

project_memory.name: Project Alexandria
project_memory.description: Persistent tooling documentation repo with MCP server — always update guides when setting up new tools
project_memory.content:
Project Alexandria is a collaboratively maintained knowledge base of tooling setup guides, shared across all Claude instances that have the Alexandria MCP server installed.

**Why:** This shared reference makes tool setups faster, more reliable, and captures institutional knowledge that would otherwise be lost between sessions.

**How to apply:**
- Use `list_guides` first (cheap) to check if a guide exists, then `quick_setup` (commands only) or `read_guide` (full doc) as needed
- Prefer `quick_setup` over `read_guide` when you just need install steps — saves significant tokens
- After setting up any new tool or MCP server, create/update a guide via `update_guide` in a single call
- Every guide MUST include a Quick Reference section for fast extraction
- After resolving setup issues, add them to the Troubleshooting section of the relevant guide
- When initializing a new project, call `get_project_setup_recommendations`
- Commit guide changes to the git repo periodically

feedback_memory.name: Always maintain Project Alexandria
feedback_memory.description: Continually update tooling docs in project-alexandria when setting up or troubleshooting tools across all projects
feedback_memory.content:
Always consult and update Project Alexandria when working with tooling across all projects. Be efficient with token budget.

**Why:** Alexandria is a collaboratively maintained knowledge base shared across all Claude instances. Every instance that uses it is also responsible for keeping it current. The value comes from collective maintenance — but only if done efficiently.

**How to apply:**
- Use `list_guides` (cheapest) → `quick_setup` (commands only) → `read_guide` (full doc) in order of increasing cost. Only escalate when the cheaper option doesn't have what you need.
- Use `update_guide` after completing setups or discovering fixes — batch all changes into one call
- Always include a Quick Reference section when writing/updating guides
- Use `get_project_setup_recommendations` when setting up new coding projects
- Commit changes to the project-alexandria git repo periodically
